# About the book {.unnumbered}

This book is not intended to be a comprehensive textbook on impact evaluation and causal inference. Many excellent books and articles already provide rigorous and detailed treatments of causal inference, program evaluation, and econometric methods for policy analysis.

The purpose of this book is different. It is to go **back to basics** and offer a simple and transparent perspective on the logic behind the most widely used impact evaluation methods. Rather than emphasizing the technical aspects of evaluation methods, the book aims to clarify how each method constructs a counterfactual, what assumptions it requires, and how it works in practice, while keeping formal details to a minimum.

## How to estimate treatment effects?

The central question of ex-post impact evaluation is simple, and it is counterfactual: **What would have happened to the outcome of interest in the absence of the treatment?** The focus is usually on the units exposed to the treatment. For these units, we observe the post-treatment outcome, but we do not observe what their outcome would have been had they not been treated. The main challenge is therefore to estimate this missing counterfactual outcome. The difference between the observed post-treatment outcome and the estimated counterfactual outcome provides an estimate of the treatment effect of interest.

![As in *Sliding Doors*, where Helen's life splits into two possible paths, causal inference starts from the fact that only one realized outcome is observed, while the alternative path remains counterfactual.](images/sliding-doors-counterfactual.png)

Crucially, **the counterfactual is never observed**. To estimate treatment effects credibly, we therefore need to construct a plausible approximation of it. How can this be done? The most common approach, though not the only one, as discussed in [Chapter 11](../chapters/11-forecast-based-designs.qmd), is to exploit information from untreated units. The basic idea is that untreated units can help approximate what would have happened to treated units in the absence of the treatment.

We will see how the most widely used evaluation strategies within this family of **control-based counterfactual methods**---randomized controlled trials (RCTs), matching methods, difference-in-differences (DiD), instrumental variables (IV), regression discontinuity (RD), and the synthetic control method (SCM)---exploit information from untreated units<!--, or from units observed under different treatment conditions,--> to retrieve the treatment effects of interest.

The central theme is **transparency**: showing how each method builds its counterfactual, from which units, and under which assumptions.

### Transparency as the Guiding Principle of This Book

In recent years, transparency has become central to the debate surrounding machine and deep learning algorithms. Complex predictive models are often difficult to interpret, and it is not always clear how an algorithm transforms data into predictions. For this reason, researchers and practitioners increasingly ask for models that are not only accurate, but also **interpretable** and **transparent**. At the same time, much less attention is usually paid to transparency in the context of impact evaluation methods. It is often taken for granted that causal methods are transparent by nature. Yet, as discussed below, this is not necessarily true for some of the most commonly used approaches.

In a successful RCT, the logic of the comparison is usually easy to understand. Random assignment makes treated and untreated units comparable before the intervention: on average, the two groups are similar in all respects except for the treatment they receive. For example, in a trial testing a new drug, patients are randomly assigned either to receive the drug or to remain in the control group, so differences in health outcomes after treatment can be credibly attributed to the drug itself. As a result, the untreated group provides a natural benchmark for what would have happened to the treated group in the absence of the intervention. Similarly, in an RD design, the role of the comparison units is relatively intuitive. Units just below and just above the cutoff are compared because they are assumed to be similar, except for treatment assignment.

This clarity is often less immediate in other widely used methods. For instance, in a matching application, it is not always disclosed which untreated units are actually used to construct the counterfactual, how much weight each of them receives, and whether all treated units are retained in the final matched sample. In a staggered DiD design, the comparison group is often described simply as “untreated”, but this label can hide considerable complexity. Applied studies do not always make clear which untreated units drive the comparison, whether not-yet-treated units are part of the control group, and how much the estimate depends on specific groups or time periods. The same issue becomes even more complex in IV designs, especially when the instrument is non-binary, when multiple instruments are used, or when the empirical specification includes several fixed effects, covariates, and trends.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Transparency

Impact evaluation methods may appear transparent at first sight. However, this apparent transparency can be misleading: the way in which they construct the counterfactual is not always clearly visible.

Understanding who is being compared with whom, and the assumptions under which the causal effects of interest can be identified, is essential for interpreting the estimates causally.
:::

**The lesson from SCM.** One of the reasons why SCM has been so influential is that it makes a data-driven construction of the counterfactual clearly visible. SCM builds the counterfactual by choosing a weighted combination of untreated units through a sophisticated optimization algorithm, and this procedure is not necessarily transparent at first sight. However, once the optimization process is completed, the method reports the weights assigned to each control unit and usually presents a graph comparing the treated unit with its synthetic counterpart. This makes it possible to see **which untreated units contributed to the counterfactual**, how much they contributed, and whether the synthetic control provides a credible pre-treatment fit---a key condition for considering the estimated treatment effect as reliable.

This feature is not only useful from a methodological point of view; it is also pedagogically powerful. It reminds us that control-based impact evaluation methods ultimately rely on comparisons between treated and untreated units. They are **not magic formulas** that automatically identify the causal effect of interest; they are structured ways of making comparisons credible under specific assumptions.

**No ground truth.** This point is particularly important because in observational studies, we do not observe a **ground truth** causal effect. We observe what happened after a policy was introduced, but we do not observe what would have happened to the same units, at the same time, in the absence of that policy. This makes it difficult to compare evaluation strategies as we often compare machine learning algorithms for prediction. In prediction problems, models can be assessed against observed outcomes, and their accuracy can be compared directly. For example, algorithms predicting the sale prices of flats can later be evaluated by comparing their forecasts with the actual prices at which those flats were sold. In impact evaluation, instead, the counterfactual outcome is always missing. We therefore cannot simply run a “horse race” among methods and declare which one is empirically more accurate. For this reason, the credibility of an impact evaluation strategy depends mainly on the plausibility of its identifying assumptions and on the transparency of the comparison it constructs.

::: {.callout-note}
## Why causality matters

Many important questions in economics, policy evaluation, business, political science, and the social sciences are **causal questions**. We do not only want to know whether two variables move together. We want to know whether changing one of them would actually change the other. This is the idea behind the familiar motto: **association is not causation**. Two variables may be strongly associated because one causes the other, but also because they are both driven by a third factor, because of selection, or simply because the observed relationship reflects a misleading comparison. Causal inference starts from this distinction.

Would a conditional cash transfer increase children’s school attendance?
Would a public subsidy reduce workplace accidents? Would smaller classes improve student achievement? Would a carbon tax reduce firms' energy consumption? In each case, the key question is not simply whether treated and untreated units differ. The key question is what would have happened to the same units in the absence of the treatment.

Estimating causal effects is difficult because this counterfactual outcome is not directly observed. We observe what happened after the policy change but not what would have happened otherwise. Impact evaluation methods are ways of making this missing comparison as credible as possible.

This is why causality is at the center of empirical work. Without a credible causal design, data can reveal patterns, correlations, and associations, but it cannot by itself tell us what works, for whom, and under which conditions.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}

## One causal effect at a time

A causal effect is defined with respect to a specific intervention, outcome, target population, and counterfactual state of the world. For this reason, causal analysis should usually begin by clarifying **one causal question at a time**.

For example, suppose the outcome of interest is workers' earnings. Asking whether a training program increases earnings is not the same as asking whether a wage subsidy, job-search assistance, or access to childcare increases earnings. The outcome is the same, but each question involves a distinct treatment, counterfactual comparison, and possibly target population. Mixing these questions in a single analysis can make the estimand unclear.

This matters because credible causal inference requires a precise answer to the question: **compared to what?** The same observed outcome may be consistent with many different causal stories. To estimate an effect, we need to define the treatment, the untreated state, the units of interest, the timing of the intervention, and the outcome to be evaluated.

Trying to estimate several causal effects at once often leads to ambiguity. It becomes difficult to understand which counterfactual is being constructed, which assumptions justify it, and what the estimated coefficient actually captures. A clear impact evaluation therefore starts by isolating one causal question, defining its counterfactual carefully, and then choosing an empirical strategy that can make that comparison credible.
:::

### The rest of the book

The second part of the book covers selected topics in impact evaluation, including:

- **Forecasting-based counterfactual methods.** These methods estimate causal effects without necessarily requiring untreated units in the data. Instead, they construct the counterfactual by forecasting what would have happened to treated units in the absence of the treatment, and therefore rely on a different set of identifying assumptions.

- **Spillovers and interference.** We will discuss what happens when the treatment affects not only treated units, but also the outcomes of some or all untreated units. In these settings, untreated units may no longer provide a 'clean' counterfactual.

- **Continuous treatments.** Throughout most of the book, we focus on **binary treatments**, where units are either treated or untreated. This setup makes the counterfactual logic especially clear: the goal is to compare outcomes under treatment with outcomes in the absence of treatment. In this chapter we will consider more complex settings in which the treatment varies in intensity. In this case, the causal question is not only whether the treatment has an effect, but how the outcome changes as treatment intensity increases.

::: {.callout-important}
## The main message

Impact evaluation is not primarily about applying sophisticated estimators. It is about constructing a credible counterfactual scenario

Different methods provide different ways of approximating the missing counterfactual, and each of them relies on specific assumptions that should be assessed **case by case**, depending on the empirical setting, the available data, and the institutional context.
:::

The book therefore does not try to cover every method, every extension, or every recent technical debate. Instead, it focuses on the basic logic of the main approaches and on the practical questions that arise when we use them to evaluate public policies.

## The audience of this book

The book is written for readers who want to understand the intuition behind impact evaluation methods before moving to technical details. It is intended for students, applied researchers, policy analysts, and practitioners who use causal methods or read studies based on them.

Formal notation is kept to a minimum and postponed to the end of each chapter. The emphasis is on **interpretation**, **design**, **credibility**, and **transparency**.

## Acknowledgments

Before taking the first steps in this journey, I would like to thank my mentor, Professor Guido Pellegrini, who taught me much of what I know about public policy evaluation. I am also grateful to my co-authors: through many discussions with them, I have refined my understanding of the discipline. Finally, I wish to thank the many curious students and public administration practitioners whose questions and insights have encouraged me to explain key concepts more clearly.

## Disclaimer

This is version 0.2 of the book. It is still a work in progress, and I would be grateful for any comments, suggestions, or corrections that could help improve it.

All illustrations in this book were created with the assistance of a generative AI system, based on specific instructions and, in some cases, hand-drawn sketches provided by the author. They are intended for illustrative and educational purposes only.

<!--# About the book {.unnumbered}

This book is not intended to be a comprehensive textbook on impact evaluation and causal inference. Many excellent books and articles already provide rigorous and detailed treatments of causal inference, program evaluation, and econometric methods for policy analysis.

The purpose of this book is different. It is to go **back to basics** and offer a simple and transparent perspective on the logic behind the most widely used impact evaluation methods. Rather than presenting these methods as technical recipes, the book aims to clarify how each method constructs a counterfactual, what assumptions it requires, and how it works in practice, while keeping formal technical details to a minimum.

## How to estimate treatment effects?

The central question of ex-post impact evaluation is simple, and it is counterfactual: **What would have happened to the outcome of interest in the absence of the treatment?** The focus is usually on the units exposed to the treatment. For these units, we observe the post-treatment outcome, but we do not observe what their outcome would have been had they not been treated. The main challenge is therefore to estimate this missing counterfactual outcome. The difference between the observed post-treatment outcome and the estimated counterfactual outcome provides an estimate of the treatment effect of interest.

Crucially, **the counterfactual is never observed**. To estimate treatment effects credibly, we therefore need to construct a plausible approximation of it. How can this be done? The most common approach, though not the only one, as discussed in Chapter X, is to exploit information from untreated units. The basic idea is that untreated units can help approximate what would have happened to treated units in the absence of the treatment.

We will see how the most widely used evaluation strategies within this family of **control-based counterfactual methods**---randomized controlled trials (RCTs), matching methods, difference-in-differences (DiD), instrumental variables (IV), regression discontinuity designs (RDD), and the synthetic control method (SCM)---exploit information from untreated units to retrieve the treatment effects of interest.

A key aspect of this discussion will be **transparency**: understanding how counterfactual scenarios are actually constructed, which units contribute to them, and under what assumptions they can be interpreted as credible approximations of what would have happened in the absence of the treatment.

### Transparency as the Guiding Principle of This Book

In recent years, transparency has become central to the debate surrounding machine learning algorithms. Complex predictive models are often difficult to interpret, and it is not always clear how an algorithm transforms data into predictions. For this reason, researchers and practitioners increasingly ask for models that are not only accurate, but also **interpretable** and **transparent**. At the same time, much less attention is usually paid to transparency in the context of policy evaluation methods. It is often taken for granted that causal methods are transparent by nature. Yet, as discussed below, this is not necessarily true for some of the most commonly used impact evaluation methods.

In a successful RCT, the gold standard of causal inference, the logic of the comparison is usually easy to understand. Random assignment makes treated and untreated units comparable before the intervention: on average, the two groups are similar in all respects except for the treatment they receive. For example, in a trial testing a new drug, patients are randomly assigned either to receive the drug or to remain in the control group, so differences in health outcomes after treatment can be credibly attributed to the drug itself. As a result, the untreated group provides a natural benchmark for what would have happened to the treated group in the absence of the intervention.

Similarly, in an RDD, the role of the comparison units is relatively intuitive. Units just below and just above the cutoff are compared because they are assumed to be similar, except for treatment assignment.

This clarity is often less immediate in other widely used methods. For instance, in matching approaches, it is not always obvious **which untreated units** are actually used to construct the counterfactual, **how much weight** each of them receives, whether they are sufficiently comparable to treated units, and whether all treated units are retained in the final matched sample.

In a DiD design, the comparison group is often described simply as “untreated”, but this label can hide considerable complexity. Applied studies do not always make clear **which untreated units drive the comparison**, whether not-yet-treated units are part of the control group, and how much the estimate depends on specific groups or time periods.

The same issue becomes even more complex in IV, especially when the instrument is non-binary, when multiple instruments are used, or when the empirical specification includes several fixed effects, covariates, and trends.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Transparency

Impact evaluation methods may appear transparent at first sight. However, this apparent transparency can be misleading: the way in which they construct the counterfactual is not always clearly visible.

Understanding who is being compared with whom, and under which assumptions, is essential for interpreting causal estimates.
:::

**The lesson from SCM.** One of the reasons why SCM has been so influential is that it makes a data-driven construction of the counterfactual clearly visible. SCM builds the counterfactual by choosing a weighted combination of untreated units through an optimization algorithm, and this procedure is not necessarily transparent at first sight. However, once the optimization process is completed, the method reports the weights assigned to each control unit and usually presents a graph comparing the treated unit with its synthetic counterpart. This makes it possible to see **which untreated units contributed to the counterfactual**, how much they contributed, and whether the synthetic control provides a credible pre-treatment fit---a key condition for interpreting the estimated treatment effect as reliable.

This feature is not only useful from a methodological point of view; it is also pedagogically powerful. It reminds us that control-based impact evaluation methods ultimately rely on comparisons between treated and untreated units. They are **not magic formulas** that automatically identify the causal effect of interest; they are structured ways of making comparisons credible under specific assumptions.

This point is particularly important because in observational studies, we do not observe a **ground truth** causal effect. We observe what happened after a policy was introduced, but we do not observe what would have happened to the same units, at the same time, in the absence of that policy. This makes it difficult to compare evaluation strategies as we often compare machine learning algorithms for prediction. In prediction problems, models can be assessed against observed outcomes, and their accuracy can be compared directly. In impact evaluation, instead, the counterfactual outcome is missing. We therefore cannot simply run a “horse race” among methods and declare which one is empirically more accurate. For this reason, the credibility of an impact evaluation strategy depends mainly on the plausibility of its identifying assumptions and on the transparency of the comparison it constructs.

### The rest of the book

The second part of the book will cover selected topics in impact evaluation, including:
- **Forecasting-based counterfactual methods.** These methods estimate causal effects without necessarily requiring untreated units in the data. Instead, they construct the counterfactual by forecasting what would have happened to treated units in the absence of the treatment, and therefore rely on a different set of identifying assumptions.
- **Spillovers and interference.** We will discuss what happens when the treatment affects not only treated units, but also the outcomes of some or all untreated units. In these settings, untreated units may no longer provide a clean counterfactual.
- **Continuous treatments.** Throughout most of the book, we focus on **binary treatments**, where units are either treated or untreated. This setup makes the counterfactual logic especially clear: the goal is to compare outcomes under treatment with outcomes in the absence of treatment. In this chapter we will consider more complex settings in which the treatment is not simply binary, but varies in intensity. In this case, the causal question is not only whether the treatment has an effect, but how the outcome changes as treatment intensity increases.

::: {.callout-important}
## The main message

Impact evaluation is not primarily about applying sophisticated estimators. It is about constructing a credible counterfactual scenario

Different methods provide different ways of approximating the missing counterfactual, and each of them relies on specific assumptions that should be assessed **case by case**, depending on the empirical setting, the available data, and the institutional context.
:::

The book therefore does not try to cover every method, every extension, or every recent technical debate. Instead, it focuses on the basic logic of the main approaches and on the practical questions that arise when we use them to evaluate public policies.

## The audience of this book

The book is written for readers who want to understand the intuition behind impact evaluation methods before moving to technical details. It is intended for students, applied researchers, policy analysts, and practitioners who use causal methods or read studies based on them.

Formal notation is kept to a minimum and postponed to the end of each chapter. The emphasis is on **interpretation**, **design**, **credibility**, and **transparency**.

Before taking the first steps in this journey, I would like to thank my mentor, Professor Guido Pellegrini, who taught me much of what I know about public policy evaluation. I am also grateful to my co-authors: through many discussions with them, I have refined my understanding of the discipline. Finally, I wish to thank the many curious students and public administration practitioners whose questions and insights have encouraged me to explain key concepts more clearly.
-->
